case_name: Natural organic matter reduction from well water for drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing natural organic matter from well water intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town operates deep wells that usually provide microbiologically stable water, but one well field shows elevated color and dissolved natural organic matter that creates taste, color, and disinfection by-product formation concerns. The utility wants a conventional, well-supported treatment approach that can be reliably operated by existing staff. Moderate capital and operating cost are acceptable, but the town prefers to avoid unnecessarily complex or high-energy systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable natural organic matter reduction sufficient to improve color and support control of disinfection by-product precursors for drinking water production.
secondary_goals: keep operation within standard municipal skill level

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: conventional sludge handling is available but large volumes of difficult brine or concentrate are undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable year-round operation with conventional municipal staffing is required

core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation: destabilize and adsorb dissolved natural organic matter onto metal hydroxide flocs
core_unit_functions.flocculation: grow settleable flocs that capture organic matter and fine particles
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: remove bulk flocculated solids and associated organic matter
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: polish remaining suspended solids and carryover floc
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological protection after precursor reduction

applicability_rationale: enhanced coagulation is a well-established mainstream process for NOM and DBP precursor reduction in drinking water treatment
the raw water has moderate TOC, UV254, and color that are compatible with coagulation-based removal
the utility prefers proven conventional technology and has manageable sludge handling available
this process chain balances performance, robustness, and operator familiarity better than more specialized alternatives
potential_risks: removal efficiency depends on coagulant dose and pH optimization
sludge production will increase compared with untreated groundwater systems
if NOM is predominantly low-molecular-weight hydrophilic material, coagulation performance may be less complete

core_unit_functions.enhanced_coagulation: remove a substantial fraction of coagulating natural organic matter
core_unit_functions.flocculation: form removable flocs carrying adsorbed organic matter
core_unit_functions.dissolved_air_flotation_or_sedimentation: separate formed solids from treated water
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: remove remaining particulate matter and floc carryover
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb residual dissolved organic matter and improve taste and odor control
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final pathogen barrier after precursor polishing

applicability_rationale: adding GAC after conventional clarification and filtration can further reduce residual NOM and DBP precursors
this is still based on established municipal treatment practice
the approach is attractive when the utility wants stronger polishing than coagulation alone
it remains more conventional and lower risk than high-pressure membranes for this case
potential_risks: GAC media replacement or reactivation adds operating cost
biological growth in GAC requires monitoring and appropriate operational control
incremental benefit may not justify cost if coagulation alone already achieves treatment goals

core_unit_functions.anion_exchange: remove dissolved organic acids and some DBP precursors through resin-based exchange mechanisms
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain resin fines and polish treated water

applicability_rationale: anion exchange is an established option for NOM and DBP precursor reduction in selected waters
the relatively low turbidity well water is favorable for a resin-based process
it can be compact and effective where dissolved organics are amenable to resin removal
it is ranked below enhanced coagulation because the utility preference is for the most conventional and widely familiar technology
potential_risks: brine or regenerant waste handling can be a significant drawback
resin fouling and competition from sulfate or other anions may affect performance
operator attention to regeneration cycles is higher than for straightforward conventional clarification

core_unit_functions.ozonation_or_biological_oxidation: transform some organic matter into more biodegradable fractions and improve downstream biological removal
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration: biodegrade biodegradable dissolved organic carbon and filter solids

applicability_rationale: biological polishing can reduce biodegradable organic fractions and improve finished water quality
the approach is known in drinking water treatment practice
it can be useful when conventional treatment needs polishing or when biologically active filters are already part of the plant
it is ranked lower because it is less direct and less universally preferred than enhanced coagulation for straightforward NOM control in a small well-water system
potential_risks: ozonation can form by-products such as bromate if bromide is present
biological filters require careful startup and operational monitoring
this route may not achieve the same direct bulk NOM removal as optimized enhanced coagulation for all source waters

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong rejection of dissolved organic matter and many other dissolved constituents
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: membranes are technically capable of substantial NOM reduction
they may be considered when multiple dissolved contaminants also require removal
for this case they are not the best-supported first choice because the problem is a relatively clear single-contaminant NOM issue and the utility prefers proven conventional treatment
potential_risks: high energy use and membrane replacement costs
concentrate management is undesirable under the stated residuals constraints
pretreatment and fouling control add complexity beyond what is needed for this easy case

preferred_rank_pattern: enhanced_coagulation_with_clarification_and_filtration should rank first for conventional NOM control
activated_carbon_polishing may rank near the top as a stronger but slightly more costly extension
high-pressure membrane options should not rank first under proven-technology preference and moderate constraints

must_reflect: preference for established municipal drinking water technologies
avoidance of unnecessarily complex or high-energy treatment for a single NOM problem
recognition that conventional sludge handling is available but difficult brine or concentrate is less desirable

must_contain: why enhanced coagulation is a standard response for NOM and DBP precursor reduction
how source-water characteristics such as TOC color and UV254 support conventional treatment selection
why membrane systems rank lower despite technical effectiveness

should_mention: coagulant dose and pH sensitivity for NOM removal
media exhaustion or biological management issues for GAC
brine or concentrate disposal concerns for ion exchange or membranes
